1: The narrative resumes with wayhî ʾaḥᵃrê-ḵēn — 'after this' — and immediately establishes David's mode of decision-making: wayyišʾal dāwiḏ baYHWH, 'David inquired of the LORD.' The idiom šāʾal baYHWH (or šāʾal bᵉ-) is the technical formula for oracle-consultation through the priestly urim and thummim (cf. 1 Sam 23:2, 4; 30:7-8), not general prayer. The inquiry yields a specific directional answer: ʿᵃlēh, 'go up' — a single-word oracle typical of the urim response (contrast with longer prophetic oracle). David then asks ʾanāh ʾeʿᵃleh, 'where shall I go up?' — a follow-up permitted by the oracle system — and receives 'Hebron' by name. The pattern: action only after divine authorization.
2: David's two wives are named: ʾAḥînōʿam hayyizrᵉʿēlît (Ahinoam the Jezreelite) and ʾAḇîgayil ʾēšet nāḇāl haKKarmᵉlî (Abigail, wife of Nabal the Carmelite). The precision of identification by geographic origin and previous marriage is a standard narrative marker; it establishes household-level claims that will matter for succession politics in ch 3.
3: The clause ʾîš û-ḇêtô — 'each man with his household' — describes a migration of extended family units, not just fighting men. The verb wayyēšᵉḇû bᵉʿārê ḥeḇrôn ('they settled in the towns around Hebron') uses the standard settlement verb yāšaḇ — to sit/dwell — marking this as permanent relocation, not a temporary encampment.
4: The anointing at Hebron: wayyimšᵉḥû šām ʾet-dāwiḏ lᵉmelek ʿal-bêt yᵉhûḏāh — 'they anointed David king over the house of Judah.' This is the second of David's three anointings in the narrative: the first was private at Bethlehem (1 Sam 16:13), this is tribal at Hebron (over Judah only), and the third will be national (2 Sam 5:3). The phrase ʿal-bêt yᵉhûḏāh specifies the scope: tribe of Judah, not all Israel — the divided kingdom is already present. The notice about Jabesh-gilead's burial of Saul inserts a note of loyalty that David will explicitly honor (vv.5-7).
5: David's message to Jabesh-gilead: ʾašer ʿᵃśîtem ḥesed im-ʾᵃḏōnêḵem ʿim-šāʾûl — 'you showed steadfast love to your lord Saul.' The noun ḥesed here designates loyal covenant action — the treatment of Saul's body as covenant-obligation to the dead king. The verb qāḇar (to bury) with reference to proper burial is a fundamental covenant-duty in the OT (cf. Deut 34:6; Josh 24:30); failure to bury is a curse (Jer 22:19). David invokes their act of ḥesed as the basis for his blessing.
6: David's blessing uses both divine and personal pronouns: wᵉgam-ʾānōḵî ʾeʿᵃśeh ʾitᵉḵem haṭṭôḇāh hazzōʾt — 'I too will repay your kindness.' The parallel — 'may YHWH show you ḥesed and faithfulness (ʾemet)... and I also will repay' — models the patron-client dynamic whereby YHWH and the king function as parallel benefactors. The word pair ḥesed wᵉʾemet (steadfast love and faithfulness) is a fixed formula in the covenant vocabulary (Exod 34:6; Ps 25:10; 89:14), applied here at the political level.
7: David's exhortation: wᵉʿattāh tiqzᵃqnāh yᵉdêḵem wihyû liḇnê-ḥāyil — 'now let your hands be strong and be courageous.' The idiom ḥāzaq yādayim (strengthen the hands) is the standard encouragement formula in commissioning speeches (Josh 1:6-9; 10:25; Hag 2:4). The death of Saul is announced not as defeat but as the occasion for renewed initiative: 'Saul your lord is dead, and also the house of Judah has anointed me king over them.' The political context is delicate — David acknowledges Jabesh's loyalty to Saul while announcing his own anointment.
8: The counter-narrative: wᵉʾaḇnēr ben-nēr śar-haṣṣāḇāʾ ʾᵃšer lᵉšāʾûl lāqaḥ ʾet-ʾîšbōšet ben-šāʾûl — 'Abner son of Ner, the commander of Saul's army, took Ishbosheth son of Saul.' The verb lāqaḥ (took, seized) implies Abner's initiative and agency — Ishbosheth is the object, not the actor. The place-name Maḥᵃnāyim ('two camps' or 'the camp of God') is east of the Jordan in Gilead — Abner has taken the heir to safety behind the river, establishing a rival power base.
9: Abner installs Ishbosheth as king over a geographic list: Gilead, the Ashurites, Jezreel, Ephraim, Benjamin, 'and all Israel.' The phrase wᵉʿal-kol-yiśrāʾēl (and over all Israel) is added at the end, implying that the enumerated regions constitute 'all Israel' — but notably Judah is absent. The contrast with v.4's ʿal-bêt yᵉhûḏāh (house of Judah) establishes the structural tension of the divided kingdom that ch 3 will eventually resolve.
10: The chronological notices serve a structural function: Ishbosheth was forty years old when he became king and reigned two years. David at Hebron reigned seven years and six months (v.11). The two timelines do not perfectly synchronize — Ishbosheth's two-year reign overlaps only partially with David's seven-and-a-half — which implies that Abner's power-consolidation preceded Ishbosheth's formal installation by several years. The precise numbers serve as anchors within the overlapping chronologies of a divided succession.
11: The notice wayhî mispar hayyāmîm ʾᵃšer hāyāh dāwiḏ melek bᵉḥeḇrôn ʿal-bêt yᵉhûḏāh šeḇaʿ šānîm wᵉšiššāh ḥᵃḏāšîm — 'the number of days that David was king in Hebron over the house of Judah was seven years and six months' — uses mispar hayyāmîm (the count of days) rather than simply šānim (years), emphasizing exactitude. This precision differentiates the Hebron regency from the later united kingship (2 Sam 5:5).
12: The civil war opens at the pool of Gibeon (berēḵat giḇʿôn). Gibeon is a significant location in Israelite memory — it is where the sun stood still for Joshua (Josh 10:12-13) and later the site of the tabernacle (1 Chr 21:29). The arrangement — Abner's men from Mahanaim facing Joab's men from David's forces — is a confrontation of two armies of the same people, a civil war staged on sacred ground.
13: The phrase wayyipgᵉšû yaḥdāw — 'they met together' — uses pāgaš, the same verb used for hostile encounters (cf. Gen 32:17; 33:8; Exod 4:24). The positioning at opposite sides of the pool (ʾēlleh ʿal-haberēḵāh mizzeh wᵉʾēlleh ʿal-haberēḵāh mizzeh) is the standard pre-battle formation. The narrator is building to the 'contest' proposal of v.14.
14: Abner's proposal: yāqûmû-nāʾ hanneʿārîm wîśaḥᵃqû lᵉpānênû — 'let the young men rise and play (śāḥaq) before us.' The verb śāḥaq means play, jest, or perform — it is used for children playing (Zech 8:5), for athletic display (Gen 26:8), but also for the Philistines' entertainment at Samson's expense (Judg 16:25). The noun mišḥāq (derived from this root) appears in v.14's summary. The word choice is darkly ironic: what begins as a 'contest' (mišḥāq — literally 'play') becomes a blood-bath in which all 24 contestants die simultaneously (v.16). The same ironic register is present when Samson is brought out to śāḥaq (Judg 16:25).
15: The matched champions: twelve for Benjamin / Ishbosheth, twelve for David. The number twelve likely reflects tribal arithmetic — each side producing one per tribe — though only Benjamin is named for Ishbosheth's side. This is not a random tournament but a ritual representative combat: the twelve are standing in for the respective kingdoms.
16: The mutual killing: wayyaḥᵃzēq ʾîš bᵉrōʾš rēʿēhû wᵉḥarbô bᵉṣad rēʿēhû wayyippᵉlû yaḥdāw — 'each man grasped his opponent's head and drove his sword into his opponent's side, and they fell together.' The verb ḥāzaq (to grasp firmly) followed by wayyippᵉlû yaḥdāw (they fell together) — simultaneous mutual destruction. The site is named Ḥelqat haSṣûrîm — 'Field of Flints' or 'Field of Sword-Edges' — a place-name whose meaning (ṣûr = rock/flint; ḥālaṣ = to draw a sword or strip) memorializes the event.
17: The battle outcome uses the niphal passive for defeat: wayyinnāgep ʾaḇnēr wᵉʾanšê yiśrāʾēl lipnê ʿaḇdê dāwiḏ — 'Abner and the men of Israel were defeated before David's servants.' The verb nāgap (to strike, smite, defeat) in the niphal — 'were smitten' — is the standard passive of military defeat, often with YHWH as the implied agent (cf. Num 14:42; Judg 20:36).
18: The introduction of the three sons of Zeruiah: Joab, Abishai, and Asahel. The comparative for Asahel — wᵉʾᵃśāhēl qal bᵉraglāyw kᵉʾaḥad hāṣᵉḇāʾyîm ʾᵃšer baśśāḏeh, 'swift of foot like one of the gazelles in the field' — uses the same animal comparison as the lament in ch.1. The ṣᵉḇî (gazelle) is the OT's standard image of agility and speed (Song 2:9, 17; 1 Chr 12:8). The detail foreshadows his death: his very swiftness is what drives him relentlessly into Abner's killing range.
19: Asahel's pursuit is characterized by the phrase lōʾ-nāṭāh lāleḵet ʿal-hayyāmîn wᵉʿal-haśśᵉmōʾl — 'not turning right or left.' This formula (from Deut 5:32; 17:11; Josh 1:7) is typically used for adherence to the Torah, not for pursuit — the ironic use here implies a single-mindedness that becomes fatal. Asahel applies the devotion of a Torah-keeper to a pursuit that Abner explicitly warns will destroy him.
20: Abner's identification of his pursuer — hᵃʾattāh zeh ʾᵃśāhēl, 'is that you, Asahel?' — and Asahel's monosyllabic answer ʾānōḵî, 'it is I/I am he,' echoes the pattern of fatal identification scenes in the narrative (cf. 2 Sam 1:8; 1 Kgs 18:7). The exchange serves as the narrative's last moment of possibility for Asahel to turn away.
21: Abner's warning: nᵉṭēh lᵉḵā ʿal-yāmînᵉḵā ʾô ʿal-śᵉmōʾleḵā — 'turn aside right or left.' This directly inverts the phrase applied to Asahel in v.19. The offer — 'seize one of the soldiers and take his equipment' (ʾᵃḥōz lᵉḵā ʾeḥāḏ mēhannᵉʿārîm wᵉqaḥ lᵉḵā ʾet-ḥᵃlîṣātô) — is a face-saving alternative: take plunder instead of pursuing a fight you cannot win. The word ḥᵃlîṣāh (armor/equipment) derives from ḥālaṣ (to strip, to equip for war).
22: Abner's second warning makes explicit the blood-feud consequence: ʾêḵ ʾeśśāʾ pānay ʾel-yôʾāḇ ʾāḥîḵā — 'how could I lift my face to Joab your brother?' The phrase nāśāʾ pānîm (to lift the face) means to face someone without shame; it implies that killing Asahel would create an obligation of blood-revenge (gōʾēl haddām, the blood-avenger) in Joab. Abner is trying to prevent the blood-feud that will eventually consume him in 3:27.
23: The killing: wayyakkēhû ʾaḇnēr bᵉʾaḥᵒrê haḥᵃnît ʾel-haḥōmeš — 'Abner struck him with the butt end of the spear into the stomach.' The phrase ʾaḥᵒrê haḥᵃnît (the hind part / butt end of the spear) specifies the non-blade end — Abner uses the shaft rather than the point, possibly attempting a non-lethal stop, but the thrust ruptures the abdomen. The noun ḥōmeš is a precise anatomical term: it appears again in 3:27 (Joab's murder of Abner) and 4:6 (murder of Ishbosheth), forming a grim pattern. In v.23 the death is accidental/defensive; in 3:27 it is premeditated murder — the same anatomical strike, different moral character.
24: The pursuit continues: wᵉyôʾāḇ wᵉʾᵃḇîšay rādᵃpû ʾaḥᵃrê ʾaḇnēr. The place-name giḇʿat ʾammāh (Hill of Ammah) is unidentified geographically; the setting sun establishes the temporal frame of the pursuit ending at dusk — a common narrative boundary marker (cf. 1 Sam 17:52; 2 Sam 3:35).
25: The Benjaminites rally around Abner: wayyitqabbᵉṣû ḇnê-ḇinyāmîn ʾaḥᵃrê ʾaḇnēr wayhyû lᵉʾaggûḏāh ʾeḥāt wayyaʿᵃmᵉḏû ʿal-rōʾš giḇʿāh ʾeḥāt — 'they formed into a single unit and stood their ground on the top of a hill.' The noun ʾaggûḏāh (unit, band, bunch) is used for a military formation and also for a bundle of hyssop (Exod 12:22) — the basic image is of things bound together. Their stand on the hilltop is a defensive tactical position.
26: Abner's appeal: hᵃlaṣṣāṇîm tōʾkal ḥereb hᵃlōʾ tēḏaʿ kî-mārāh tihyeh bāʾaḥᵃrônāh — 'Must the sword keep killing forever? Do you not know that this will end in bitterness?' The adverb laṣṣāṇ (forever/endlessly) intensifies the rhetorical question. The noun mārāh in kî-mārāh tihyeh means bitterness or distress — Abner is projecting the cost of continued civil war. His rhetorical question ʿad-mātay lōʾ tōʾmar lāʿām lāšûḇ, 'when will you tell the people to stop pursuing their brothers?' introduces the kinship bond — ʾaḥîḵem, 'their brothers' — as the reason for restraint.
27: Joab's oath: ḥay hāʾĕlōhîm kî lûlēʾ dibbartā kî-ʾāz min-habboqer niʿᵃlāh hāʿām — 'As God lives, if you had not spoken, the men would have kept up the pursuit until morning.' The oath particle ḥay hāʾĕlōhîm (as God lives) is the standard assertion of truthfulness by appeal to the living God. Joab's counter-assertion is that Abner's earlier bellicosity at Gibeon (v.14) was itself the provocation — framing the entire day's violence as Abner-initiated.
28: The trumpet signaling ceasefire: wayyitqaʿ yôʾāḇ baSšôpār wayyaʿᵃmᵉḏû ḵol-hāʿām — 'Joab blew the trumpet and all the people stopped.' The šôpār (ram's horn) is the standard military signal instrument; in the law codes it also marks Jubilee (Lev 25:9) and covenant assembly (Exod 19:16). Its sounding ends the battle — wᵉlōʾ yōsîpû ʿôḏ lirdōp ʾaḥᵃrê yiśrāʾēl, 'they no longer pursued Israel' — but the narrative closure is explicitly limited: the war continues in 3:1.
29: Abner's night march through the Arabah: wᵉʾaḇnēr wᵉʾᵃnāšāyw hālaḵû bāʿᵃrāḇāh kol-hallayla hahûʾ. The Arabah is the Jordan rift valley — a difficult terrain requiring a full night of travel. wayyaʿᵃḇᵉrû ʾet-hayyardēn, 'they crossed the Jordan' — back to safety at Mahanaim east of the river. The Bithron (habbiṯrôn, 'the gorge/ravine') is otherwise unidentified but likely a geographic feature in Gilead.
30: The casualty report for David's side: nineteen men plus Asahel missing from Joab's force (niqlam meʿaḇdê dāwiḏ tišʿāh ʿāśār ʾîš wᵉʾᵃśāhēl). The specificity of nineteen — with Asahel named separately — indicates an accurate accounting based on an actual muster. The asymmetry with v.31 (360 Benjaminite dead) establishes David's military advantage even at this early stage.
31: David's forces struck down 360 of Benjamin/Abner's men — the number is exact and memorable. The specificity likely reflects an archival source. In contrast, Joab's side loses only 19 + Asahel. The lopsided ratio (roughly 18:1) establishes military superiority for David's forces even at this stage of the civil conflict.
32: The burial of Asahel in his father's tomb at Bethlehem (bᵉḇêt leḥem bᵉqeḇer ʾāḇîw) closes the episode. Proper burial in the ancestral tomb is the mark of a good death; Asahel receives this honor despite dying in civil war. Joab and Abishai then marched through the night to Hebron — closing the civil war episode with a return to base, not triumph.
